answer:
**In Mirza Ghalib's *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, 'aaina' (mirror) is a recurring metaphor symbolizing reflection, self-knowledge, beauty's revelation, the illusory or tarnished nature of existence, and the interconnectedness of hearts and the cosmos.**[^1][^2][^3]

It often reveals harsh truths that deflate pride or ego, as in the opening couplet of one ghazal where beholding one's face in the mirror ends all boasting.[^4] Ghalib extends this to the heart and world as mirrors: "From sun to atom, heart reflecting heart is a mirror; / For the mimic soul, the world from every part is a mirror."[^5] Here, creation—from celestial bodies to particles—mirrors inner realities, echoing Sufi ideas of divine reflection in all things.

Mirrors also evoke love's spectacle or pain: one suggests offering a mirror so the beloved's beauty becomes a "show" others admire.[^6] Yet perfection tarnishes—"A mirror's flawless wonder finds a stain at last"[^7]—paralleling how love, youth, or clarity fades. The poet even likens himself to quicksilver (the mirror's backing), intensifying reflection's "heat."[^8]

Ghalib weaves 'aaina' into themes of wonder (*hairat*), illusion, and transience, but the corpus offers no singular prose definition—only these poetic uses across ghazals.[^9]

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 12 ("Az mehr ta-ba-zarra dil-o-dil hai aaina"), [translation/](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/12-az-mehr-ta-ba-zarra-dil-o-dil-hai-aaina/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., ch. 176 ("Safaa-e-hairat-e-aaina hai saamaan-e-zang aakhir"), [translation/](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/176-safaa-e-hairat-e-aaina-hai-saamaan-e-zang-aakhir/translation/).

[^3]: Ibid., ch. 178 ("Seemab-pusht garmi-e-aaina de hai hum"), [translation/](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/178-seemab-pusht-garmi-e-aaina-de-hai-hum/translation/).

[^4]: Ibid., ch. 4 ("Aaina dekh apna sa munh le ke reh gaye"), [p-354db2](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/04-aaina-dekh-apna-sa-munh-le-ke-reh-gaye/translation/#p-354db2).

[^5]: Ibid., ch. 12, [p-93900f](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/12-az-mehr-ta-ba-zarra-dil-o-dil-hai-aaina/translation/#p-93900f).

[^6]: Ibid., ch. 5 ("Aaina kyun na doon ke tamasha kahein jise"), [p-8dd888](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/05-aaina-kyun-na-doon-ke-tamasha-kahein-jise/translation/#p-8dd888).

[^7]: Ibid., ch. 176, [p-e27dd2](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/176-safaa-e-hairat-e-aaina-hai-saamaan-e-zang-aakhir/translation/#p-e27dd2).

[^8]: Ibid., ch. 178, [p-5c4ec0](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/178-seemab-pusht-garmi-e-aaina-de-hai-hum/translation/#p-5c4ec0).

[^9]: Search of *Diwan-E-Ghalib* (239 chapters) yields ~5 ghazals with 'aaina' in matla/radif; no explicit philosophical treatise.
